title: Building an Inverted Index with Postings Lists
body: An inverted index maps each normalized term to a postings list of documents. The pipeline tokenizes text, builds a term dictionary, records term frequency, and sorts document identifiers so query processing can intersect postings efficiently.

title: Positional Postings for Phrase Search
body: A positional index stores the exact token positions where a term occurs. Phrase queries such as information retrieval are answered by checking whether positions for adjacent terms appear consecutively in the same document.

title: Tokenization and Term Normalization
body: Index construction starts with tokenization, lowercasing, stopword filtering, and light stemming. These choices define the term vocabulary and determine whether queries like ranking and ranked retrieve the same documents.

title: Dynamic Index Updates for Small Search Engines
body: A small search engine can maintain an in-memory index and periodically write the dictionary and postings to disk. Dynamic updates add new documents, remove deleted identifiers, and rebuild summary statistics such as average document length.

title: Query-Time Candidate Generation
body: Candidate generation starts by taking the union or intersection of postings for query terms. A ranked retrieval engine then scores only candidate documents instead of scanning the entire corpus.

title: Boolean Retrieval with AND OR and NOT
body: Boolean retrieval treats queries as set expressions over postings lists. AND computes an intersection, OR computes a union, and NOT subtracts a set from the document universe.

title: Phrase Search as a Hard Filter
body: A phrase search requires all quoted terms to occur in the specified order. SearchLens applies phrase constraints before scoring so a document must contain the exact phrase to be ranked.

title: Proximity Matching Beyond Exact Phrases
body: Proximity ranking rewards documents where query terms occur near each other. This can improve user satisfaction for verbose queries because related terms in the same passage usually signal topical relevance.

title: Limitations of Pure Boolean Search
body: Pure Boolean search returns an unordered set, so users must inspect every matching document. Ranked retrieval addresses this limitation by ordering results with tf-idf, BM25, or other scoring functions.

title: Parsing User Queries into Search Operators
body: A query parser recognizes parentheses and operators, normalizes ordinary terms, and builds an executable expression. Even a compact parser can support AND, OR, NOT, and simple precedence rules.

title: BM25 Ranking with Term Frequency Saturation
body: BM25 ranks documents by combining inverse document frequency with a saturated term frequency component. Repeating a query term helps at first, but the gain decreases so long documents do not dominate unfairly.

title: Document Length Normalization in BM25
body: The BM25 b parameter controls document length normalization. A higher value penalizes documents that are much longer than the collection average, while k1 controls the strength of term frequency saturation.

title: Why BM25 is a Strong Lexical Baseline
body: BM25 is a durable lexical baseline because it is simple, efficient, and robust across many ad hoc retrieval collections. Modern hybrid systems often compare dense retrieval against BM25 before adding reranking.

title: BM25 Implementation Notes
body: A practical BM25 implementation needs document frequency, document length, average length, and term frequency statistics. Scores can be computed over a candidate set from the inverted index.

title: BM25 Failure Cases
body: Lexical BM25 may miss relevant documents that use different vocabulary from the query. Query expansion, thesauri, dense retrieval, or user feedback can reduce this vocabulary mismatch problem.

title: Vector Space Ranking with TF-IDF
body: The vector space model represents documents and queries as weighted term vectors. TF-IDF combines term frequency with inverse document frequency, and cosine similarity compares the angle between vectors.

title: Inverse Document Frequency and Specificity
body: Inverse document frequency assigns higher weights to terms that occur in fewer documents. Specific terms help discriminate relevant documents, while very common terms contribute less to ranking.

title: Cosine Normalization for Different Document Lengths
body: Cosine normalization divides by vector length so longer documents do not automatically receive higher scores. This is useful when corpus documents vary in length and verbosity.

title: Comparing TF-IDF and BM25
body: TF-IDF cosine and BM25 are both lexical ranking models. BM25 adds saturation and length normalization, while tf-idf cosine offers a simple geometric interpretation.

title: Term Weighting in a Complete Search System
body: A complete scoring system often starts with tf-idf term weighting, candidate generation, and efficient top-k ranking. Engineering decisions determine latency, memory use, and reproducibility.

title: Crawler Politeness and Robots Exclusion
body: A web crawler should respect robots.txt, throttle requests to each host, and avoid overloading servers. Politeness policies are essential for responsible collection of web pages.

title: URL Frontier Scheduling
body: The URL frontier stores discovered links and selects the next page to fetch. Good scheduling balances freshness, host politeness, priority, and coverage of important pages.

title: Duplicate and Near-Duplicate Detection
body: Crawlers often encounter mirrored pages and repeated templates. Fingerprinting and shingling can detect near duplicates before they waste index space and distort retrieval evaluation.

title: Parsing HTML into Indexable Text
body: A crawler must extract readable text from HTML, remove boilerplate navigation, preserve useful anchor text, and pass clean content to the indexing pipeline.

title: Freshness in Incremental Crawling
body: Incremental crawling revisits pages according to expected change frequency. Freshness is especially important for news, prices, schedules, and other dynamic search collections.

title: PageRank and the Random Surfer Model
body: PageRank models a random surfer following links with occasional teleportation. A page receives high score when many important pages link to it.

title: Combining Link Analysis with Text Ranking
body: Web search systems can combine content relevance from BM25 with link authority from PageRank. The combined score improves ranking when anchor text and links indicate popularity or trust.

title: Anchor Text as External Evidence
body: Anchor text often describes the destination page using words chosen by other authors. Indexing anchor text can improve retrieval for pages whose own content lacks the query terms.

title: Topic-Sensitive Link Ranking
body: Topic-sensitive PageRank computes authority within topical subsets. It can help distinguish pages about search engines from pages about unrelated popular subjects.

title: Link Spam and Authority Manipulation
body: Link-based ranking can be manipulated by link farms and spam networks. Robust systems monitor suspicious link patterns and combine authority with content quality signals.

title: Edit Distance for Spelling Correction
body: Spelling correction ranks candidate terms by edit distance from the misspelled query term. Insertions, deletions, substitutions, and transpositions approximate the effort needed to fix a word.

title: K-Gram Indexes for Tolerant Retrieval
body: A k-gram index stores character n-grams for each vocabulary term. It generates candidates for wildcard queries and spelling correction before edit distance reranking.

title: Noisy Channel Ranking for Corrections
body: A noisy channel spelling model combines the likelihood of an observed typo with the prior probability of the intended term. Frequent vocabulary terms receive more confidence after candidate generation.

title: Context-Sensitive Query Correction
body: Context-sensitive correction uses surrounding query words to choose among valid alternatives. For example, search engine technolgy should become search engine technology rather than a different rare word.

title: When Not to Correct a Query
body: A search system should avoid aggressive correction for names, acronyms, and technical terms such as BM25 or nDCG. Showing a suggestion is safer than silently rewriting a specialized query.

title: Rocchio Relevance Feedback
body: Rocchio feedback moves a query vector toward relevant documents and away from nonrelevant documents. It is a classic method for improving vector space retrieval when relevance judgments are available.

title: Pseudo Relevance Feedback without User Labels
body: Pseudo relevance feedback assumes the top-ranked documents from an initial search are relevant. Expansion terms are selected from those documents and appended to the query.

title: Thesaurus-Based Query Expansion
body: A thesaurus can add synonyms and related concepts to a query. This helps with vocabulary mismatch but can reduce precision if broad terms introduce topic drift.

title: Term Selection for Feedback
body: Feedback term selection favors high-idf terms that occur frequently in top documents. Filtering stopwords and original query terms keeps expansion concise.

title: Risks of Topic Drift
body: Query expansion can drift when the first-pass results include nonrelevant documents. Conservative feedback depth and a small number of expansion terms reduce the risk.

title: Precision Recall and F-Measure
body: Evaluation begins by comparing retrieved documents with relevance judgments. Precision measures the fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant, while recall measures coverage of all relevant documents.

title: Mean Average Precision
body: Mean average precision summarizes the precision observed at every rank where a relevant document appears. MAP rewards systems that retrieve relevant documents early and consistently.

title: nDCG for Graded Relevance
body: Normalized discounted cumulative gain evaluates ranked lists with graded relevance. Highly relevant documents receive larger gain, and the discount factor rewards early ranks.

title: TREC-Style Test Collections
body: A test collection contains a corpus, topics, relevance judgments, and evaluation metrics. This structure supports reproducible comparison among retrieval models.

title: Limitations of Offline Evaluation
body: Offline metrics may not capture user satisfaction, presentation quality, or interactive search behavior. A deployed system should combine test collection results with user-centered evaluation.

title: Variable Byte Encoding for Postings
body: Variable byte encoding stores small gaps with fewer bytes. Search engines gap-encode sorted document identifiers before compression to reduce postings list size.

title: Gamma Codes and Integer Compression
body: Gamma codes encode positive integers using a unary length and binary offset. They are useful for compact postings but require bit-level processing.

title: Skip Pointers for Faster Intersections
body: Skip pointers accelerate postings intersection by allowing query processing to jump over blocks that cannot contain a match. They trade extra storage for faster Boolean evaluation.

title: Dictionary Compression
body: The term dictionary can be compressed with blocked storage and front coding. Compression improves memory locality for large vocabularies.

title: Compression Trade-Offs in Search Engines
body: Index compression reduces disk and memory footprint but increases decoding cost. Practical systems balance latency, storage, and implementation complexity.

title: Dense Retrieval with Learned Embeddings
body: Dense retrieval encodes queries and documents into continuous vectors. Approximate nearest neighbor search retrieves semantically similar documents even when exact query terms are absent.

title: Hybrid Lexical and Dense Retrieval
body: Hybrid retrieval combines BM25 lexical scores with dense vector similarity. The lexical component preserves exact matching, while dense retrieval can address synonymy and paraphrases.

title: Cross-Encoder Reranking
body: A cross-encoder reranker reads the query and document together to estimate relevance. It is often more accurate than first-stage retrieval but too expensive to run over the whole corpus.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
body: Retrieval-augmented generation uses a search component to supply evidence to a language model. The quality of retrieved passages directly affects answer faithfulness.

title: Evaluating Dense Retrieval Against BM25
body: Dense systems should be compared with BM25 baselines on the same queries and relevance judgments. Without a lexical baseline, semantic retrieval improvements are hard to interpret.

title: Search Spam and Keyword Stuffing
body: Search spam attempts to manipulate ranking by repeating popular terms, hiding text, or creating low-quality pages. Robust ranking should detect unnatural term distributions.

title: Adversarial Documents in Retrieval
body: Adversarial documents can exploit lexical matchers by including many query terms without satisfying the information need. Evaluation should include difficult negative examples.

title: Trust Signals for Web Search
body: Trust signals include domain reputation, link quality, author information, and content consistency. They complement textual relevance in open web environments.

title: Bias and Fairness in Ranking
body: Ranking systems can amplify exposure disparities when popular documents receive more clicks and authority. Fairness-aware evaluation studies whether different groups receive equitable visibility.

title: Robust Evaluation of Search Systems
body: Robust retrieval evaluation tests performance under misspellings, spam, ambiguous queries, and distribution shift. Stress tests reveal limitations not visible in easy benchmark topics.
